content.text: Architecture Health Scorecards for GenAI Systems

Architecture reviews without a shared measurement framework produce inconsistent outcomes: two reviewers examining the same system may reach opposite conclusions about its health. An architecture health scorecard converts subjective review into a set of observable, weighted signals that produce a reproducible overall score, identify the specific gaps that drive it down, and track improvement over successive review cycles.

For GenAI systems, scorecards must cover concerns that do not appear in traditional application architecture reviews: model version staleness, prompt injection surface, token cost efficiency, LLM-specific observability, and semantic cache effectiveness. This document defines a five-dimension scorecard tailored to enterprise GenAI platforms.

attrs.alt: GenAI architecture health scorecard — five dimension cards with scores, checks, and priority actions

content.text: Why Scorecards Rather Than Checklists

A checklist answers the question 'did we do this?'. A scorecard answers 'how well are we doing, compared to last month and compared to our peers?'. For architecture governance the distinction matters because:
Trend visibility: a score of 82 this month vs. 74 last month tells the story that a binary pass/fail checklist cannot.
Priority clarity: weighted dimensions surface which gaps have the most impact on the overall score, so teams invest effort where it matters.
Leadership communication: a single number per dimension is more legible to non-engineers than a 40-item checklist, without hiding the detail that engineers need.
Regression detection: a quarterly score drop from GREEN to YELLOW triggers investigation even when no individual metric has crossed a threshold.
The Five Scorecard Dimensions
Each dimension groups related architectural signals into a 0–100 score. The overall health score is a weighted sum of the five dimensions.
Dimension
Weight
What it measures
Reliability
Availability SLO compliance, circuit breakers, fallback providers, retry logic, warm pools
Security
Input scanning, output DLP, RBAC on tool use, audit logging, tenant isolation, secret handling
Cost Efficiency
Semantic cache hit rate, model routing tiering, prompt compression, cost-per-request vs. target
Observability
Distributed trace coverage, token usage metrics, prompt logging, alert coverage, SLO burn rate dashboards
Maintainability
ADR coverage, tech debt backlog age, model version pinning, dependency freshness, documentation currency

Security carries the highest weight (30%) because a single unmitigated security gap in a GenAI system can expose user data or enable prompt injection at scale — consequences that outweigh the cost impact of a cache miss or a stale dependency.
Scoring Model Implementation

Each dimension is scored by evaluating a set of checks. Checks are binary (present/missing) or graduated (0%, 50%, 100%), and each carries a weight within its dimension. The dimension score is the weighted average of its check scores, scaled to 0–100.
Example scorecard for a GenAI chat platform

scorecard = HealthScorecard(
    system_name="GenAI Chat Platform",
    review_date="2025-07-01",
    dimensions=[
        Dimension("Reliability", 0.25, [
            Check("Availability SLO ≥ 99.9%",      0.30, "pass"),
            Check("Circuit breaker on LLM calls",   0.25, "pass"),
            Check("Fallback provider configured",   0.20, "fail"),
            Check("Retry with exponential backoff", 0.15, "pass"),
            Check("Warm pool for burst handling",   0.10, "partial"),
        ]),
        Dimension("Security", 0.30, [
            Check("Input injection scanning",       0.20, "pass"),
            Check("Output DLP filter",              0.25, "fail"),
            Check("RBAC enforcement on tool calls", 0.20, "pass"),
            Check("Immutable audit log",            0.20, "fail"),
            Check("Tenant data isolation",          0.15, "pass"),
        ]),
        Dimension("Cost Efficiency", 0.15, [
            Check("Semantic cache hit rate ≥ 30%",  0.30, "pass"),
            Check("Tiered model routing",           0.30, "pass"),
            Check("Prompt token compression",       0.20, "pass"),
            Check("Cost-per-request within target", 0.10, "pass"),
            Check("Budget alerts configured",       0.10, "partial"),
        ]),
        Dimension("Observability", 0.15, [
            Check("Distributed traces on all paths",0.25, "pass"),
            Check("Token usage metrics exported",   0.25, "pass"),
            Check("Prompt/response logging",        0.20, "partial"),
            Check("Alert coverage > 90% of errors", 0.20, "pass"),
            Check("SLO burn rate dashboard",        0.10, "pass"),
        ]),
        Dimension("Maintainability", 0.15, [
            Check("ADR coverage > 80% of decisions",0.25, "pass"),
            Check("No tech debt > 90 days old",     0.25, "fail"),
            Check("All model IDs version-pinned",   0.20, "pass"),
            Check("No dependency > 2 major versions behind", 0.20, "partial"),
            Check("Runbook for all P1 alerts",      0.10, "pass"),
        ]),
    ]
)

scorecard.print_summary()

# === GenAI Chat Platform Architecture Health (2025-07-01) ===
#   Reliability          [████████░░]  82.5  YELLOW
#   Security             [██████░░░░]  67.0  RED
#   Cost Efficiency      [█████████░]  91.0  GREEN
#   Observability        [████████░░]  87.5  GREEN
#   Maintainability      [███████░░░]  73.8  YELLOW
#   OVERALL              score=79.3  YELLOW
#
#   Priority Actions:
#     1. [Security/P1] Output DLP filter: fail
#     2. [Security/P1] Immutable audit log: fail
#     3. [Reliability/P2] Fallback provider configured: fail
#     4. [Maintainability/P2] No tech debt > 90 days old: fail
#     5. [Security/P2] ... (next highest-impact gap)
Check Definitions and Evidence Requirements

Each check must have an unambiguous, verifiable definition to prevent reviewers from applying different standards. The following table defines pass/partial/fail criteria for the security dimension checks, which carry the most governance weight.
Check
PASS criteria
PARTIAL criteria
FAIL criteria
Input injection scanning
Every user-supplied string passed to an LLM prompt passes through a dedicated scanner with Unicode normalisation and known-pattern detection
Scanner present but not applied to all input paths (e.g. file uploads not scanned)
No scanner; raw user input interpolated into prompts
Output DLP filter
All LLM responses pass through a DLP filter before reaching the client; filter covers PII regex patterns and secret patterns
DLP present for some response paths but not all
No DLP filter on LLM output
RBAC on tool calls
Every tool invocation checks caller permissions via a centralised policy; deny-by-default
RBAC present but some tools lack permission checks
Tools invoked without permission checks
Immutable audit log
Every LLM request and response, with user ID, timestamp, model, and token count, is written to an append-only log with hash-chain integrity
Audit log present but not append-only or missing required fields
No audit log of LLM interactions
Tenant data isolation
Vector store queries and context assembly are scoped to the authenticated tenant; cross-tenant data leakage tested in CI
Isolation implemented but not covered by automated tests
No tenant isolation; shared vector store without scoping
Automating Scorecard Generation

Many checks can be evaluated automatically by querying infrastructure APIs, scanning configuration files, and running probe requests. A monthly CI job generates the scorecard and opens a GitHub issue if the overall score falls or any dimension drops to RED.

# .github/workflows/arch-health-scorecard.yml
name: Architecture Health Scorecard

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 8 1 * *"   # 1st of every month, 08:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  scorecard:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install httpx pyyaml

      - name: Run automated checks
        env:
          OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_API_KEY }}
          DATADOG_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.DD_API_KEY }}
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: python scripts/arch_health_check.py --output scorecard.json

      - name: Upload scorecard artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: arch-scorecard
          path: scorecard.json

      - name: Open issue if RED or declining
        if: always()
        run: python scripts/scorecard_alerting.py --scorecard scorecard.json
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          SLACK_WEBHOOK: ${{ secrets.SLACK_ARCH_WEBHOOK }}
Automated checks that can run without manual review
Model version pinning: grep all config files for mutable model aliases (e.g. 'gpt-4' without a date suffix); fail if any are found.
Dependency freshness: run pip-audit and compare installed versions against latest; count how many are more than one major version behind.
Circuit breaker presence: static analysis for LLM client calls not wrapped in a resilience decorator or equivalent.
Audit log coverage: query the audit log storage for records in the last 24h; check count vs. expected traffic volume.
Alert coverage: query Datadog/PagerDuty for the ratio of monitored error types to total error types in traces.
Cost vs. target: query the cost API for the last 30 days and compare against the budgeted amount per-feature.
Trend Tracking and Historical Comparison

A single scorecard snapshot is useful. A time series of scorecards is essential for detecting slow degradation and measuring the impact of improvement initiatives. Store each monthly scorecard as a JSON artefact committed to the repository.

import json
from pathlib import Path

SCORECARD_DIR = Path("docs/architecture/scorecards")

def load_history(system: str, last_n: int = 6) -> list[dict]:
    files = sorted(SCORECARD_DIR.glob(f"{system}-*.json"))[-last_n:]
    return [json.loads(f.read_text()) for f in files]

def print_trend(system: str) -> None:
    history = load_history(system)
    print(f"\n=== {system} — 6-month trend ===")
    print(f"  {'Month':10s}  {'Overall':8s}  {'Security':9s}  {'Reliability':12s}")
    for h in history:
        month = h["review_date"][:7]
        overall = h["overall_score"]
        security = next(d["score"] for d in h["dimensions"] if d["name"] == "Security")
        reliability = next(d["score"] for d in h["dimensions"] if d["name"] == "Reliability")
        # Arrow indicates trend vs. previous month
        trend = "→" if len(history) == 1 else ("↑" if overall > history[0]["overall_score"] else "↓")
        print(f"  {month:10s}  {overall:5.1f} {trend}   {security:5.1f}      {reliability:5.1f}")
Integrating Scorecards into the Review Cycle
The scorecard feeds into each ritual in the architecture review cadence:
Ritual
Scorecard usage
Monthly health review
Primary input: current scorecard is the agenda driver. RED dimensions get dedicated time; GREEN dimensions get a 2-minute confirmation.
Quarterly strategy session
6-month trend chart presented at the retrospective block; declining dimensions become Q+1 investment priorities.
ADR review
ADRs that address a RED or YELLOW check reference the scorecard dimension explicitly; approved ADRs are expected to move the relevant check to PASS.
Annual audit
Full year of scorecards reviewed; long-term trends across all dimensions inform the next year's platform investment roadmap.

A scorecard that is generated but not discussed in a meeting is waste. Tie each scorecard publication to a scheduled review where at least two engineers commit to reading it before the session. The meeting exists to decide what to do about the score — not to read it together for the first time.
Summary

Architecture health scorecards convert qualitative review into reproducible, weighted scores across five dimensions: Reliability (25%), Security (30%), Cost Efficiency (15%), Observability (15%), and Maintainability (15%).
Security carries the highest weight because a single unmitigated gap can expose user data or enable prompt injection at production scale.
Each check has explicit PASS/PARTIAL/FAIL criteria to prevent reviewers applying different standards — ambiguous checks produce meaningless scores.
Many checks can be automated via CI: model version pinning, dependency freshness, audit log coverage, circuit breaker presence, and cost vs. target.
Store monthly scorecard JSON artefacts in the repository and compute 6-month trend lines to detect slow degradation before it reaches RED.
The scorecard feeds directly into the monthly health review (agenda driver), quarterly strategy session (trend retrospective), and ADR review (impact traceability).
